channel.title: Security-Research on Robert Akhmerov | v31dt
channel.description: Recent content in Security-Research on Robert Akhmerov | v31dt

item.title: AI Hackbot Scaffolding
item.description:
This is a follow-up to my podcast episode, Future of Bug Bounty and How to Build Your Own HackBot. After that conversation, I kept thinking about the practical question behind the whole idea: now that the models are getting so strong, how much scaffolding do you actually need?

My current conclusion for black-box bug bounty and security testing is this:

A small, sane scaffold gets you around 60-80% of the practical benefit of a much bigger system.

item.title: Creativity Needs Structure
item.description:
This is the second part of a connected pair of posts. The first one is about learning through systems.

I used to think about creativity as something that happens when structure disappears. The more freedom, the more original the result. That sounds attractive, but I do not think it is true anymore.

Total freedom is often not creative. It can become vague. If there are no constraints, there is nothing to push against, no direction, and no reason to choose one path over another. A blank page can look like freedom, but it can also be a trap.

item.title: Insecure Deserialization Research Workflow
item.description:
Insecure deserialization doesn’t get the attention it deserves.

Finding the sink is one thing, but the actual painful part is usually exploitability: building the gadget / POP chain and proving the payload actually works in that specific runtime. That’s the part that used to eat most of the researcher time.

I think AI opens up a pretty big opportunity here. Not really as some magic “generate exploit” button, but to automate a lot of the chain discovery process: finding reachable gadgets, killing dead paths, reasoning about object/property constraints, and validating candidates.

item.title: ELK + Burp Suite Monitoring System
item.description:
Context

This project built a Burp Suite and ELK Stack setup for centralized storage, analysis, and anomaly detection of HTTP requests.

Project report: Anomaly Detection and Historical Analysis of Burp Suite Requests

Technologies

  • Burp Suite
  • Elasticsearch
  • Logstash / Beats
  • Kibana
  • Filebeat
  • HTTP request analysis

Result

The setup supports security research and vulnerability tracking by collecting and analyzing HTTP traffic in a centralized monitoring stack.

Reflection

This project connected penetration testing workflows with monitoring and analysis. It pushed me to think beyond individual findings and look at how request data can be stored, searched, and used during longer security investigations.
